unitTitle: Unit 1: Present simple, present continuous, stative verbs

content.subtitle: Examples
items: Marsha **goes** to dance lessons every Saturday.
I **like** the new James Bond film.

content.subtitle: Helpful hints
content.description: The present simple is often used with frequency adverbs and routine expressions.
items: These adverbs usually go before the verb, but after the verb **be**.
I **often play** football with my friends.
I am **often** late for my piano lessons.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
notes: The verbs **be** and **have** have irregular present forms.

rows.label: negative
rows.right: He/she/it **is not (isn't / 's not) playing** ... You/we/they **are not (aren't / 're not) playing** ...

content.subtitle: Examples
items: She **is working** at the museum until the end of the month.
My brother **is always borrowing** my CDs without asking!

content.subtitle: Helpful hints
content.description: The present continuous is commonly used with words that show something is happening around now.
items: We can use **always** + present continuous to show irritation about repeated behaviour.
My brother **is always borrowing** my CDs without asking.

content.subtitle: Form
content.description: Stative verbs do not usually describe actions. They describe states (feelings, thoughts, etc). They are not normally used in continuous tenses.
items: ✓ I **like** reading books in my free time.
✗ I **am liking** reading books in my free time.

content.subtitle: Helpful hints
content.description: Stative verbs usually describe a state, not an action, so we normally use simple tenses.
items: Common groups: feelings (**love, hate, prefer**), thinking (**know, believe, understand**), possession (**have, belong to**) and perception (**seem, appear**).
Use simple forms for states: I **know** the answer. / She **has** two sisters.
Some verbs can be both stative and action verbs depending on meaning.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
notes: Some stative verbs (such as **be**, **have** and **think**) are used in continuous tenses when they describe actions.
What do you **think** about his new song?
